title: A New Friend

story.scene: Outside the same Madrid café, Carlos introduces you to his longtime friend Ana.
story.mission: Today you’ll learn to tell Ana where you’re from, say where you live, and ask the same questions back.
story.continuity: You reuse your Lesson 1 greeting and name before learning two new place phrases. Ana and Carlos invite you to the Lesson 3 family dinner.

emotionalArc.startsWith: The learner is meeting a new Spanish speaker for the first time.
emotionalArc.endsWith: The learner knows they can introduce themselves to someone new.
emotionalArc.earnedBy: Telling Ana where they are from and live, then asking her in return.

microCliffhanger.speaker: Carlos
microCliffhanger.spanish: Esta noche vienes a cenar. Quiero presentarte a mi familia.
microCliffhanger.english: Tonight you’re coming for dinner. I want to introduce you to my family.

carlosIntroduction.text: ¡Hola de nuevo! You already know how to greet someone and share your name. Today I’m introducing you to my longtime friend Ana. Tell her where you’re from, say where you live now, and ask her the same questions. Listen to the conversation first—then I’ll show you the two useful patterns inside it.

canDo: Introduce yourself using language from Lesson 1.
Say where you are from with soy de.
Say where you live with vivo en.
Ask another person where they are from.
Keep the exchange moving with ¿y tú?
objectives: Introduce yourself using language from Lesson 1.
Say where you are from with soy de.
Say where you live with vivo en.
Ask another person where they are from.
Keep the exchange moving with ¿y tú?

essentialPhrases.usage: Use soy de for your city, region, or country of origin.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Me llamo Tom. Soy de Canadá.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: My name is Tom. I’m from Canada.

essentialPhrases.usage: Use vivo en for the place where you live now.
essentialPhrases.exampleSpanish: Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: I’m from Canada and I live in Winnipeg.

essentialPhrases.usage: A natural informal question after exchanging names.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: Nice to meet you, Ana. Where are you from?

essentialPhrases.usage: Ask this when the conversation turns from origin to someone’s current home.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: You’re from Mexico. Where do you live now?

essentialPhrases.usage: Return the same question without repeating the whole sentence.
essentialPhrases.exampleEnglish: I live in Madrid. And you?

essentialPhrases.usage: Use this when you share the same origin, home, or other detail.

vocabulary.tip: Literally means I call myself. It is the safest everyday way to introduce your name.
Use soy for identity, name, origin, profession, and nationality.
Correct and clear, but more formal or official than me llamo.
Use with friends, peers, children, or relaxed first meetings.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to address respectfully.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Good morning, what is your name?

vocabulary.tip: Use in casual conversation after names are exchanged.
Respectful version for formal introductions.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, where are you from?

vocabulary.tip: Add a city, region, or country after de.
Use vivo en for where you live now, not necessarily where you are from.
Use after names are exchanged. It is natural across the Spanish-speaking world.
Use encantado if the speaker is male. Use encantada if the speaker is female.
Use encantada when the speaker is female.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Pleased to meet you, I am Lucía.

vocabulary.tip: Use after answering to invite the other person to answer too.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I am from Canada. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Formal version of ¿y tú?
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I live in Winnipeg. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Use to add yourself to a statement.
Useful when saying you live here or are here with someone.
Use with de or en when talking about places.
Useful when talking about origin or nationality.
Use soy de Canadá for origin and vivo en Canadá for residence.
A useful country name for conversations with Carlos.
Formal you. It uses forms like es and se llama.
Informal you. Use with peers and relaxed situations.
A natural reply to mucho gusto or encantado.

introductionPhrases.whenUsed: Me llamo is clear and safe. Soy plus your name is shorter and common in relaxed introductions. Encantado changes to encantada if the speaker is female.
Use mi nombre es in official, classroom, or very formal situations. In normal conversation, me llamo sounds warmer.
Use with peers, friends, children, or relaxed first meetings.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, and respectful introductions. The usted can be included for clarity or omitted when context is obvious.
Use after a greeting and name exchange in casual conversation.
Use in respectful or professional introductions.
Use soy de plus a city, region, or country to talk about origin.
Use vivo en for where you live now. It is different from soy de, which talks about origin.
Mucho gusto works everywhere. In Spain, encantado or encantada is also very natural.
Use informally after answering a name, origin, or residence question. Use y usted in formal situations.
Use formally after answering a question from someone you address with usted.

grammar.topic: Soy de for origin and vivo en for residence
grammar.meaning: Separate where you come from from where you live now.
grammar.explanation: Use soy de + place for your origin. Use vivo en + place for your current home. Learn these as two complete patterns: soy de Canadá; vivo en Winnipeg. You do not need any other forms of the verbs yet.

notes.title: Origin
notes.text: Soy de Canadá means I’m from Canada. Keep de before the place.

notes.title: Current home
notes.text: Vivo en Winnipeg means I live in Winnipeg. Keep en before the place.

notes.title: Keep the contrast clear
notes.text: Soy en Winnipeg is not correct for residence, and vivo de Canadá is not correct for origin. Match soy with de and vivo with en.

examples.spanish: Soy de Canadá, pero vivo en Winnipeg.
examples.english: I’m from Canada, but I live in Winnipeg.

grammar.practicePrompt: Say one true sentence about where you are from and one about where you live.

lines.english: Tom, this is my friend Ana.

lines.english: Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: I am from Canada. And you?

lines.english: Carlos, where are you from?

lines.english: I am from Mexico. And you?

lines.spanish: Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg.
lines.english: I am from Canada and I live in Winnipeg.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg. ¿Cómo te llamas?
lines.english: Hi, my name is Tom. I am from Canada and I live in Winnipeg. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Me llamo Carlos. Soy de Madrid. Mucho gusto, Tom.
lines.english: My name is Carlos. I am from Madrid. Nice to meet you, Tom.

dialogue.title: Meeting Ana outside the café
dialogue.setting: After your first conversation, Carlos introduces you to his longtime friend Ana outside the same Madrid café.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: ¡Hola! Tom, esta es mi amiga Ana.
lines.english: Hi! Tom, this is my friend Ana.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.spanish: Hola, me llamo Ana. Mucho gusto. ¿Cómo te llamas?
lines.english: Hi, my name is Ana. Nice to meet you. What’s your name?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: My name is Tom. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.english: Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg. ¿Y tú?
lines.english: I’m from Canada and I live in Winnipeg. And you?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.spanish: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo en Madrid.
lines.english: I’m from Seville, but I live in Madrid.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Yo soy de Madrid y vivo aquí también. Ana, cenamos con mi familia mañana. ¿Vienes?
lines.english: I’m from Madrid and I live here too. Ana, we’re having dinner with my family tomorrow. Are you coming?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.english: Of course! See you tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: See you tomorrow.

comprehension.prompt: What are Ana and the learner invited to?

supplementalPronunciation.focus: Names, origin phrases, formal questions, and smooth linking.
tips: In many Latin American accents, ll in llamo sounds like English y.
Keep nombre as two clear parts: nom-bre. Do not add an extra vowel at the end.
Soy is one short syllable. Do not stretch it.
In ¿de dónde?, keep de light and stress dón naturally.
Usted ends with a clear d sound, but it can be soft in natural speech.
In soy de, connect the words smoothly without stressing de.
Encantado and encantada stress ta: en-can-TA-do, en-can-TA-da.

items.note: Connect the words smoothly; many speakers say ll like y.
Keep nombre crisp; avoid saying nom-buh-ray.
One short syllable, with a clear oy sound.
Link soy de naturally; de is short and light.
Listen for de plus donde; do not drop de.
Practice the final d gently, not like a strong English d.
Link vivo en smoothly, but keep both words clear.

tips: Keep de light in soy de.
Link vivo en smoothly while keeping both vowels clear.

items.note: Connect soy de as one short phrase.
Link vivo en without dropping either vowel.
Keep de light and stress dón in dónde.
Let the question rise gently at the end.

items.text: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo en Madrid.
items.meaning: I’m from Seville, but I live in Madrid.
items.note: Pause lightly around pero to separate origin from residence.

listening.title: Origin or current home?
listening.instructions: Listen for the complete chunks soy de and vivo en before choosing the meaning.

items.spanish: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo aquí.
options: I’m from Seville, but I live here.
items.answer: I’m from Seville, but I live here.

options: I live in Madrid. And you?
items.answer: I live in Madrid. And you?

listeningPhrases.note: Listen for the smooth me llamo.
Notice that soy can introduce a name.
Listen for te, which makes it informal.
Listen for se, which makes it formal here.
Listen for de before the place.
Listen for vivo en, not soy de.

listeningPhrases.english: Hi, I am Tom. And you?
listeningPhrases.note: Natural connected speech with a follow-up question.

speaking.instructions: Repeat each place phrase, recall the questions, then personalize the complete exchange.

items.prompt: Repeat the origin phrase, then use your own place.
items.focus: Keep soy de connected as one short phrase.

items.prompt: Repeat the residence phrase, then use your own city.
items.focus: Link vivo en smoothly without dropping either vowel.

items.prompt: Ask Ana where she is from without reading the model.
items.focus: Stress dón in dónde and let the question rise naturally.

items.prompt: Say where you are from and where you live in one response.
items.text: Soy de Canadá, pero vivo en Winnipeg.
items.meaning: I’m from Canada, but I live in Winnipeg.
items.focus: Pause lightly around pero to separate the two ideas.

items.prompt: Say where you live and return the question naturally.
items.focus: Stress quí in aquí and tú in the return question.

miniConversation.setting: Carlos introduces you to Ana outside the café.
miniConversation.goal: Exchange names, origin, and current home without reading a full script.

turns.speaker: Ana
turns.spanish: Hola, me llamo Ana. ¿Cómo te llamas?
turns.english: Hi, my name is Ana. What’s your name?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Say your name and use mucho gusto.
turns.exampleEnglish: My name is Tom. Nice to meet you.

turns.speaker: Ana
turns.english: Nice to meet you. Where are you from?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Say where you are from, where you live, and return the question.
turns.exampleSpanish: Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg. ¿Y tú?
turns.exampleEnglish: I’m from Canada and I live in Winnipeg. And you?

turns.speaker: Ana
turns.spanish: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo en Madrid.
turns.english: I’m from Seville, but I live in Madrid.

turns.speaker: Carlos
turns.spanish: Ana, cenamos con mi familia mañana. ¿Vienes?
turns.english: Ana, we’re having dinner with my family tomorrow. Are you coming?

turns.speaker: Learner
turns.intent: Accept the invitation and close with a Lesson 1 phrase.
turns.exampleEnglish: Yes, thank you. See you tomorrow.

culture.title: Introductions set the tone
culture.text: After exchanging names, mucho gusto works naturally across the Spanish-speaking world. People often answer ¿de dónde eres? with a city or region instead of only a country. In Spain, someone may have two surnames, one from each parent, but friendly introductions normally begin with a first name.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers introduce themselves
notes: After hola from Lesson 1, many speakers go straight to soy plus their name in relaxed settings.
Me llamo remains completely natural and clear in first introductions.
¿Cómo se llama? often sounds more natural than always adding usted, as long as the formal context is clear.
People often answer origin with a city: Soy de Guadalajara, Soy de Winnipeg.
¿Y tú? and ¿y usted? are simple ways to keep a first conversation from stopping.

commonMistakes.explanation: Me llamo literally means I call myself. The me is required.

commonMistakes.correction: Use soy de for origin and vivo en for where you live.
commonMistakes.explanation: Soy de Canadá means I am from Canada. Vivo en Canadá means I live in Canada.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using ¿cómo se llama? with a friend when you mean informal you.
commonMistakes.correction: Use ¿Cómo te llamas? with friends and peers.
commonMistakes.explanation: ¿Cómo se llama? is formal when speaking directly to someone.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting de in soy de Canadá.
commonMistakes.explanation: Spanish needs de before the place of origin.

commonMistakes.mistake: Translating What is your name? word-for-word.
commonMistakes.correction: Say ¿Cómo te llamas? or ¿Cómo se llama?
commonMistakes.explanation: Spanish uses the idea What do you call yourself? for this question.

commonMistakes.correction: Say Soy Tom or Soy de Canadá.
commonMistakes.explanation: Yo is correct, but Spanish often omits it because the verb already shows who is speaking.

reviewNotes.summary: You can now introduce yourself, ask someone's name, say where you are from, say where you live, and keep the conversation going with ¿y tú? or ¿y usted?
essentials: Name: Me llamo Tom. / Soy Tom.
selfCheck: Can I introduce myself after saying hola?
Can I ask a name formally and informally?
Can I explain the difference between soy de and vivo en?
Can I return a question with ¿y tú? or ¿y usted?
Can I speak to Carlos for 30 seconds using at least five lesson phrases?
reviewNotes.nextStep: Now that you can introduce yourself, the next lesson will help you introduce the people closest to you.

supplementalQuizBank.prompt: What does me llamo mean?
Choose the natural casual way to say I am Tom.
How do you ask What is your name? informally?
What does mucho gusto mean?
Which phrase means I am from Canada?
Choose the formal way to ask What is your name?
What is the difference between soy de and vivo en?
options: Soy de is your name; vivo en is your job.
They mean exactly the same thing.
Soy de is origin; vivo en is where you live.
Vivo en is only for countries.
supplementalQuizBank.answer: Soy de is origin; vivo en is where you live.

supplementalQuizBank.prompt: A friend asks: ¿De dónde eres? Choose the best answer.
Which phrase is formal?
Choose the correct fill-in: Vivo _____ Winnipeg.
You meet Carlos' older neighbor. Choose the safest first question.
Choose the most natural short introduction after Hola.
Carlos says: Me llamo Carlos. Soy de Madrid. Choose the best response.
Which sentence avoids overusing yo?
options: Yo soy Tom y yo soy de Canadá.
Soy Tom y soy de Canadá.
Yo soy Tom y soy yo de Canadá.
supplementalQuizBank.answer: Soy Tom y soy de Canadá.

supplementalQuizBank.prompt: Complete the conversation: Soy de Canadá. _____?
Translate: ¿Cómo te llamas?
Translate: Soy de México.
Me _____ Tom.
Soy _____ Canadá.
You want to return a question formally. What do you say?

quiz.explanation: Use soy de + place to state your origin.
Use vivo en + place for your current home.

quiz.prompt: Which question means Where are you from?
Which question means Where do you live?
quiz.explanation: ¿Dónde vives? asks about someone’s current home.

quiz.prompt: Ana asks: ¿De dónde eres? Choose the best answer.
quiz.explanation: Answer an origin question with soy de + place.
The residence pattern is vivo en + place.
The origin pattern is soy de + place.

quiz.prompt: Which sentence correctly separates origin from residence?
options: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo en Madrid.
Vivo de Sevilla, pero soy en Madrid.
Soy en Sevilla y vivo de Madrid.
Me llamo Sevilla y soy Madrid.
quiz.answer: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo en Madrid.
quiz.explanation: Use soy de for origin and vivo en for residence.

quiz.prompt: How do you return Ana’s question informally?
quiz.explanation: ¿Y tú? naturally asks the same question back to a friend or peer.

quiz.prompt: Ana says she also lives in Madrid. What can Carlos say?

quiz.explanation: The complete origin question is ¿de dónde eres?
Vivo en means I live in.
Soy de identifies someone’s place of origin.

quiz.prompt: Ana says: Soy de Sevilla, pero vivo en Madrid. What does she mean?
options: She is from Seville and now lives in Madrid.
She is from Madrid and now lives in Seville.
She works in Seville and studies in Madrid.
quiz.answer: She is from Seville and now lives in Madrid.
quiz.explanation: Soy de gives Ana’s origin; vivo en gives her current home.

quiz.prompt: Choose the most complete introduction to Ana.
options: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg. ¿Y tú?
quiz.answer: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg. ¿Y tú?
quiz.explanation: This reuses a greeting and name, adds origin and residence, and returns the question.

speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenos días, mi nombre es Tom. Mucho gusto.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Say where you are from and return the question informally.
Have a short introduction conversation with Carlos using at least five target phrases.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canadá y vivo en Winnipeg. ¿Cómo te llamas? Mucho gusto, Carlos. ¿Y tú?

realLifeMission.title: Carlos Challenge: Meet Ana
realLifeMission.mission: Carlos introduces you to Ana. Greet her, exchange names, say where you are from and where you live, then ask about her origin.
successCriteria: Reuse a greeting and name phrase from Lesson 1.
Say your origin with soy de + place.
Say your current home with vivo en + place.
Ask ¿de dónde eres? or return the question with ¿y tú?
Close with mucho gusto, nos vemos, or hasta luego.
realLifeMission.completionResponse: ¡Muy bien! You met Ana, shared where you’re from and where you live, and kept the conversation moving.
realLifeMission.correctionRule: Celebrate the completed introduction first. Correct only one soy de or vivo en error that changes the meaning.
realLifeMission.handoff: Esta noche vienes a cenar. Quiero presentarte a mi familia.

canDo: I can say where I am from.
I can say where I live now.
I can ask someone where they are from.
I can combine Lesson 1 greetings with a longer introduction.
review.conversationReturn: At the family dinner, greet Ana again and naturally reuse me llamo, soy de, vivo en, mucho gusto, and ¿y tú?

flashcards.selectionRule: The core deck reinforces only the chunks needed to exchange identity, origin, and residence.

passportStamp.title: New Friend
passportStamp.description: You met Ana and exchanged where you are from and where you live.
passportStamp.unlockCondition: Complete the Carlos Challenge for Lesson 2.

achievement.title: First Conversation
achievement.description: Have your first extended introduction with another Spanish speaker.
